artifacts.content:
Subject: Papá’s close call + why I’m so hung up on 8th Street

Hi Mamá,

I wanted to explain a little more about why I’ve been so intense about all these sidewalk projects lately, especially around downtown and near my building at 1427 Q.

Remember last year when Papá was almost hit in his wheelchair crossing 8th and Q early in the morning? It was March 15, 2023, a Wednesday. You called me right after, and I left the office and walked the whole stretch from 8th & Q back toward my apartment between about 7:00 and 8:30 a.m. I had my little notebook and wrote down every missing curb ramp, every broken panel, and the spots where the slope is way too steep for his chair.

I keep thinking about that morning when I’m working on the 8th Street Pedestrian Safety and Sidewalk Infill Project and the other ADA fixes nearby. It’s not just abstract “users” to me anymore; it’s Papá, it’s the older folks in my building, it’s Luis and Camila trying to cross to the bus stop at 8th & P without having to step into the street.

Work has been extra busy because I’m trying to keep everything within the existing right-of-way so we can move fast. It’s all small stuff on paper—curb ramps, sidewalk infill, a little trenching and backfilling—but when you’re the one pushing a walker or a stroller, it’s huge.

Anyway, I just wanted you to know I’m not being dramatic when I nag you about making sure Papá doesn’t rush at those crossings. I still see that intersection in my head every time I bike past it.

Los quiero mucho,
Maria

metadata.subject: Papá’s close call + why I’m so hung up on 8th Street

artifacts.content:
Subject: Luis’s near-miss + Dad’s fall + this 8th St project

Hey Eli,

Catching up while the kids are finally in bed and I’m pretending the laundry pile doesn’t exist.

I don’t think I ever told you the full story about why I’m so emotionally wrapped up in the 8th Street Pedestrian Safety and Sidewalk Infill Project. On March 14, 2024, Luis was walking home from Washington Elementary and was almost hit at 8th & H. Some guy rolled the stop sign and Luis had to jump back. Another parent saw it and walked him the rest of the way. I met them on the corner and I swear my hands were shaking for an hour.

Then there’s Papá. Remember his email last May after his podiatry appointment at Sutter? On May 11, 2023 he almost went down at the northeast corner of 8th & I because the curb there is a mess and there’s no tactile warning strip. That’s part of why I insisted we include six new ADA-compliant curb ramps with the sidewalk infill between G and I.

I walk that same corridor every weekday after dropping Camila at her before-school program at Cesar Chavez, between about 7:45 and 8:00 a.m., heading toward 915 I. That’s also where that creepy guy followed me and yelled threats near the unstriped crosswalk at 8th & H back in March 2022. So yeah, it’s personal.

On the bright side, construction should only be 6–8 weeks, and when it’s done, it’ll be safer for all of us—kids, Papá with his walker, and me in the mornings.

Call me this weekend if you’re free.

Besos,
Mare

metadata.subject: Luis’s near-miss + Dad’s fall + this 8th St project

artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick check on 8th St traffic impacts

Hi Jason,

Hope your week’s going better than mine. I’m knee-deep in the 8th Street Pedestrian Safety and Sidewalk Infill Project again and wanted to sanity-check something with you.

As you know, we’re filling in about 420 linear feet of sidewalk on the east side of 8th between G and I, putting in the six new ADA ramps at the northeast corners of 8th & G, 8th & H, and 8th & I, plus the high-vis crosswalks. The problem is the contractor is telling us they’ll need to shut down one travel lane in each direction for several weeks during construction. I’m fully expecting that to cause noticeable delays in the peak hours, especially with everyone trying to get to the County buildings and the kids coming through from Washington Elementary.

From a practical standpoint, it still feels like a minor enough change that we can treat it as part of the usual temporary construction disruption, but I wanted your read on whether we really need a full-blown traffic study or if we can rely on standard detour/flagging plans and call it good.

Selfishly, I’m also thinking about my own routine—dropping Camila at Cesar Chavez before school and then walking past 8th & H to 915 I, plus Miguel’s every-other-Friday 5:30 p.m. custody exchanges with the kids along that stretch between G and I. I’d love to make sure the traffic control plan doesn’t make things worse for kids and parents trying to cross.

Can we grab 15 minutes this week to talk it through?

Thanks,
Maria

metadata.subject: Quick check on 8th St traffic impacts

artifacts.content:
Title: Personal project + CEQA thoughts – 8th St / Q Street / 8th & P

Date: 2024-06-18

– Personal motivation I need to remember (for myself, not for any official write-up): Luis’s near-miss at 8th & H on 3/14/24 walking home from Washington Elementary; Dad’s almost-fall at 8th & I on 5/11/23 after his Sutter podiatry appointment; the creepy March 2022 incident when that guy followed me half a block near the unstriped crosswalk at 8th & H while I was walking from Camila’s before-school at Cesar Chavez to the office at 915 I.

– Also can’t forget the morning of 3/15/23 when Dad was almost hit in his wheelchair at 8th & Q. I walked the block in front of 1427 Q Street from 7:00–8:30 a.m. and documented every non-compliant ramp and missing segment. That’s when this all stopped being abstract.

– Kids’ bus stop at 8th & P: I timed the construction window (July 8–Aug 23, 2024) so the worst of the sidewalk work there happens while school’s out. Still need to coordinate with Miguel about Luis and Camila’s schedule so they’re not standing in a construction zone at 7:15 a.m.

– CEQA brain dump: Work really is just small-scale sidewalk and curb ramp upgrades in existing ROW, but I’m also aware we’re leaning on the categorical exemptions partly because a full initial study/EIR would be a time and budget sink the City doesn’t have. Need to keep language tight and focused on safety and existing urban context.

– Note to self: don’t drag specific family incidents into any public-facing docs, even if they’re what keep me pushing this.

metadata.title: Personal project + CEQA thoughts – 8th St / Q Street / 8th & P

artifacts.content:
Title: Custody exchange – Miguel picks up kids (8th St corridor)

Time: Friday 2024-08-09, 5:30–6:00 p.m.
Location: 8th Street between G & I, Sacramento (east side)
Attendees: Maria, Miguel, Luis, Camila

Notes:

– Court-ordered exchange (case 19FL02387) – every other Friday at 5:30 p.m. Miguel drives in from Roseville and usually pulls over along the east side of 8th between G and I. Right now it’s that awful patch with the missing sidewalk where we’re doing the 8th Street Pedestrian Safety and Sidewalk Infill Project (approx. 420 linear feet of new sidewalk plus ramps and high-vis crosswalks).

– Short-term logistics: Check with the contractor earlier in the week to see which intersections will be torn up. If the northeast corners at 8th & G, 8th & H, or 8th & I are closed for curb ramp work, plan to meet Miguel on the G Street side or at the Washington Elementary parking lot instead so the kids aren’t climbing over cones.

– Long-term: Once the sidewalk and ramps are in, this will actually be a decent, visible waiting area for the kids instead of them standing in the dirt shoulder with cars flying by. Try to remember that when I’m annoyed at the detours.

– Reminder: Bring Luis’s Chromebook and Camila’s stuffed llama for the weekend. Confirm with Miguel about their bus stop at 8th & P once school starts back up and the July 8–Aug 23 construction window is done.

artifacts.content:
Subject: Draft 8th St project blurb – can you take a quick look?

Hi Priya,

I’m trying to get a clean internal summary together for tomorrow’s coordination meeting with the design team on the 8th Street Pedestrian Safety and Sidewalk Infill Project and could use your eyes.

Here’s the gist I’ve got so far: we’re filling in about 420 LF of missing sidewalk on the east side of 8th between G and I, putting in six ADA-compliant ramps at the northeast corners of 8th & G, 8th & H, and 8th & I, minor utility adjustments, plus the new high-visibility crosswalks and signage.

I also added a bullet about replacing the existing big street trees with smaller ornamental species. I know that sounds terrible from an environmental perspective, but Urban Forestry keeps telling us those mature trees are wreaking havoc on the pavement and businesses are constantly complaining about leaf litter and root heave. The idea is to swap them for something that won’t destroy the new sidewalk in ten years.

For context, this is the same corridor where Luis had that near-miss at 8th & H last March walking home from Washington Elementary, where Dad almost went down at 8th & I after his May 11, 2023 Sutter appointment, and where I walk every morning after dropping Camila at Cesar Chavez on my way to 915 I. So I’m trying to balance my “tree hugger” side with the reality that the sidewalk itself needs to be functional and safe.

If you have five minutes to mark up the language this afternoon, I’d really appreciate it.

Thanks,
Maria

metadata.subject: Draft 8th St project blurb – can you take a quick look?

artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick CEQA sanity check – 8th St sidewalk package

Hi Carlos,

I’m polishing up the internal CEQA memo for the 8th Street sidewalk/curb ramp package (the one that includes the fixes in front of my building at 1427 Q and the stretch between G and I) and wanted to run a couple of thoughts by you before I send it up the chain.

I’m framing it under Class 1, 3, and 4 – all work in existing public ROW, minor trenching/backfilling, small new sidewalk segments, etc. The only thing giving me mild heartburn is that when you add up all the little pieces (8th & Q, 8th between G and I, the bus stop at 8th & P, plus the curb/gutter replacement where we had those trip-and-fall complaints from the elderly tenants at 1427 Q), we’re probably nudging past the usual “tiny project” disturbed area that people think of with these exemptions.

That said, you and I both know we’ve treated similar-scale sidewalk/ADA bundles as exempt in the past without anyone batting an eye, especially in fully built-out downtown blocks. I’m planning to emphasize that there’s no capacity expansion, no change in drainage patterns, and that this is really about cleaning up existing safety/liability issues.

On a personal note, it still feels weird to be writing so clinically about corners where my dad almost fell after his Sutter appointment and where my sister slipped near 8th & R back in 2019. But that’s the job, I guess.

Can you skim my draft this afternoon and let me know if anything raises a red flag for you?

Thanks,
Maria

metadata.subject: Quick CEQA sanity check – 8th St sidewalk package

artifacts.content:
Title: 8th St coordination mtg prep – design + cumulative impacts

Date: 2024-05-10

Agenda thoughts for meeting with Engineering Services:

– Confirm final layout of approximately 420 LF of new sidewalk on east side of 8th between G and I; make sure high-vis crosswalks line up with desire lines from Washington Elementary and Cesar Chavez.

– Double-check details on the six ADA ramps at NE corners of 8th & G, H, I – slopes, tactile warnings, drainage. Remember Dad’s near-fall at 8th & I after his May 11, 2023 Sutter podiatry appointment; don’t let them cut corners there.

– Ask about schedule for 8th & P bus stop work (kids’ bus stop) – July 8–Aug 23 window still realistic? Need to coordinate with Miguel so Luis and Camila aren’t dodging excavators at 7:15 a.m.

– New LED message signs: need to push back a bit. Design wants billboard-style changeable message boards near the intersection that will definitely be visible from the adjacent apartments. From a CEQA standpoint I’m treating this as part of the overall minor improvements package, but I know we’re adding a little bit to nighttime light and traffic/air quality in a corridor that’s already busy. Individually it’s small, but cumulatively it’s one more straw on the camel’s back.

– Remind myself: keep the formal write-up focused on how tiny this is relative to existing downtown conditions, and that we’re not adding lanes or new development, just cleaning up the pedestrian environment I walk through every weekday between 7:45 and 8:00 a.m. on my way to 915 I.

metadata.title: 8th St coordination mtg prep – design + cumulative impacts

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Hernandez <mhernandez@sacramento.gov>
To: Self <mhernandez.personal@gmail.com>
Date: May 3, 2024 4:42 PM
Subject: Notes – 8th & R exemption memo (finish Monday)

Draft thoughts while they’re fresh, so I don’t forget what I did today.

This afternoon I finally wrapped the CEQA exemption memo for the 8th Street and R Street curb ramp/sidewalk work. I dated it May 3, 2024. I ended up leaning on Class 1 (existing facilities) and Class 4 (minor alterations to land) because the project is strictly confined to replacement of existing sidewalk and curb ramps in the public right‑of‑way, no expansion of capacity, no new alignment, nothing beyond the disturbed footprint.

In “Reasons Why Project Is Exempt,” I referenced the 2019 internal safety audit that flagged that corner after my younger sister, Elena Martinez, slipped on the heaved slab near 8th and R. I noted that her slip‑and‑fall incident triggered a minor workers’ compensation claim through her employer, which is part of why Risk has been quietly nudging Public Works to prioritize that location. I kept it factual but neutral, since it’s technically an internal document.

Important: make sure the version for the public file keeps the audit reference but omits anything that could be tied back to Elena personally or her workers’ comp claim. The internal safety audit reference in the exemption memo is fine, but I don’t want anyone connecting “planner Maria Leticia Hernandez” to “Elena Martinez – sister” in a way that looks like favoritism.

Reminder for Monday: send clean PDF of the signed memo to Engineering Services Division project folder and cc City Attorney’s office.
